blurb: Japanese home classic - beef grilled with sweet-savoury soy glaze.

prov.popular_in: Japanese households; izakayas; worldwide teriyaki popularity
prov.famous_for: The Japanese sweet-savoury glaze that became a global favourite
prov.consists_of: Beef grilled with teriyaki sauce (soy, mirin, sugar, ginger)
prov.name_origin: Japanese teriyaki (teri=gloss, yaki=grill); cooking technique name
prov.why_loved: Sweet-savoury glaze, caramelised edges, tender beef, quick cooking
prov.method_detail: Grill - beef marinated, grilled, brushed with teriyaki glaze until caramelised

brief.lead: A weeknight Japanese favourite - sirloin or ribeye steaks marinated in a soy-mirin glaze, seared in a smoking-hot pan until caramelised and glossy. Serve immediately over steamed rice with a side of miso soup and pickled vegetables.
expect: Sear in smoking-hot pan for caramelisation
Teriyaki sauce reduces to sticky glaze
Slice against grain for maximum tenderness
kit: Large frying pan or grill pan

tl: Combine soy, mirin, sugar, ginger, garlic in pan, simmer 2 min
Cool sauce, coat beef, rest 20 min at room temperature
Sear 2-3 min per side in smoking-hot pan
Brush with reduced sauce, rest 5 min
Slice against grain, serve over rice
steps: Make the teriyaki sauce
Combine soy sauce, mirin, sugar, ginger, garlic and black pepper in a small saucepan. Simmer for 2 minutes until sugar dissolves, then cool completely.
Cooking the sauce mellows the garlic and ginger while dissolving the sugar.
Marinate the beef
Place beef in a shallow dish, pour cooled teriyaki sauce over, turning to coat. Let stand at room temperature for 20 minutes.
Room temperature meat sears evenly and stays tender.
Sear the steaks
Heat a large frying pan or grill pan over high heat until smoking. Remove beef from marinade (reserve sauce). Sear steaks 2-3 minutes per side for medium-rare.
High heat creates the caramelised crust essential to teriyaki.
Glaze and rest
Return reserved marinade to pan, simmer 1 minute until syrupy. Brush over steaks. Remove to a board, rest loosely tented for 5 minutes.
Resting redistributes juices for tender meat; glaze adds glossy finish.
Slice and serve
Slice beef against the grain into thin strips. Serve immediately over steamed rice with remaining sauce drizzled over, plus miso soup and pickles.
Slicing against grain maximises tenderness; rice balances the salty-sweet glaze.

blurb: Lamb chops grilled over coals, served with vibrant Argentine chimichurri sauce.

prov.famous_for: The perfect pairing - grilled lamb and herbaceous chimichurri
prov.consists_of: Lamb chops grilled over coals, served with parsley-garlic chimichurri sauce
prov.name_origin: South African braai tradition with Argentine chimichurri influence
prov.why_loved: Smoky char from coals, vibrant herb sauce, meltingly tender lamb
prov.method_detail: Braai - lamb chops marinated, grilled over hot coals, served with chimichurri

brief.lead: A fusion favourite - well-trimmed lamb chops marinated in olive oil and garlic, grilled over hot coals until charred and smoky, served with a bright, herbaceous chimichurri sauce. The sauce cuts through the rich lamb with vinegar and fresh herbs, while open-fire cooking adds that signature South African braai flavour. Serve with pap, grilled vegetables or a fresh salad.
expect: Grilled over hot coals for smoky char
3-4 minutes per side for pink, tender lamb

tl: Blend herbs, garlic, oil, vinegar, spices, rest 10 min
Coat chops in oil-garlic mix, rest 20 min
Heat coals until medium-hot (you can hold hand 5cm above for 3 sec)
Sear 3-4 min per side over medium-hot coals
steps: Make the chimichurri sauce

Place parsley, garlic, olive oil, red wine vinegar, oregano, salt, pepper and chilli flakes in a blender. Pulse until roughly chopped but still textured. Pour into a bowl and rest for 10 minutes to meld flavours.
Resting allows herbs and garlic to infuse the oil and vinegar.
Marinate the lamb
Mix olive oil, minced garlic, salt and pepper in a shallow dish. Coat lamb chops thoroughly, turning to cover both sides. Let stand at room temperature for 20 minutes.
Marinating adds flavour and helps the lamb sear, not steam.
Prepare the braai
Build a medium-hot fire - you should be able to hold your hand 5cm above the coals for 3 seconds. Rake the coals into an even bed.
Medium heat allows fat rendering without burning the exterior.
Grill the chops
Place lamb on the braai grid over medium-hot coals. Grill for 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare to medium, turning once. The chops should develop a rich, charred crust.
Open-fire cooking creates the signature smoky flavour of a braai.
Rest and serve
Remove chops to a board, loosely tent with foil, and rest for 5 minutes. Serve with chimichurri spooned generously over, alongside pap, potato wedges or grilled vegetables.
Resting redistributes juices for tender meat; chimichurri's acidity cuts the richness.

blurb: South Africa's daily bread - maize meal porridge, from crumbly phutu to smooth slap pap, the base of every meal.

prov.popular_in: Every South African home, across all cultures
prov.famous_for: Being the universal staple that accompanies stews, braais and curries nationwide
prov.consists_of: Maize meal cooked with water and salt into a stiff porridge, ranging from crumbly (phutu) to smooth (slap pap)
prov.name_origin: 'Pap' is Afrikaans for porridge; maize meal was introduced by Dutch traders and became the staple crop
prov.why_loved: Humble, filling and endlessly versatile - the foundation of South African cuisine
prov.method_detail: Stovetop - water brought to boil, maize meal added while stirring, simmered 20-30 min until desired texture, stiff for phutu, softer for slap pap

brief.lead: The foundation of South African cooking - fine maize meal cooked with salted water into a smooth, thick porridge. For phutu (crumbly pap), cook to a stiff texture that breaks apart in soft mounds. For slap pap (smooth porridge), add more water and cook longer until silky. Serve immediately as the perfect accompaniment to curries, stews and braais, or with milk and sugar for breakfast.
expect: Cooked entirely in a single heavy pot
Stiff phutu or smooth slap pap
Best served hot, straight from the stove

tl: Bring salted water to rolling boil in heavy pot
Pour in steady stream while stirring constantly
Cook 5 min over low heat, stirring occasionally
Cover and cook 10-15 min for phutu (20 min for slap pap)
Beat with wooden spoon until smooth, serve hot
steps: Bring water to boil
Place water and salt in a heavy-based pot and bring to a rolling boil over high heat. A heavy pot prevents scorching.
Boiling water prevents lumps when maize meal is added.
Add the maize meal
Pour maize meal into the boiling water in a steady stream with one hand while stirring vigorously with the other hand using a wooden spoon.
Stirring while adding prevents lumps from forming.
First simmer
Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 5 minutes, stirring once or twice to prevent sticking.
Initial cooking softens the maize meal grains.
Second cook for texture

For phutu (stiff, crumbly pap), cook another 10-15 minutes until very thick and pulls away from the sides. For slap pap (smooth, soft porridge), add 125ml more water and cook another 15-20 minutes until silky.
Longer cooking breaks down grains for desired texture.
Beat and serve
Remove from heat and beat vigorously with the wooden spoon until smooth and glossy. Serve immediately in soft mounds alongside stews, curries or braai meats, or with milk and sugar for breakfast.
Beating aerates the pap for lighter texture; serves best hot.
